text: The camera is introduced into the surgical field, providing a view of the port setup. The setup includes a thirty-degree down camera positioned to the left of the umbilicus, avoiding the falciform ligament.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to dissect through the gastrohepatic omentum, revealing the parasophageal hernia. The hernia is gently reduced using the robotic fourth arm, taking care not to tear the serosa of the stomach.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to dissect the right crus of the diaphragm, carefully navigating the space between the muscle and the hernia sac. No significant branches of the left hepatic artery are visible, allowing the surgeon to proceed without preservation.

The surgeon begins to identify the plane between the hernia sac and the crus of the diaphragm, revealing a thin separation layer. The hernia sac is gradually reduced, which is crucial for managing large paraesophageal hernias.

The surgeon identifies the junction where the right and left crura of the diaphragm meet posteriorly. The left crus of the diaphragm is being dissected using bipolar cautery. The table side assistant pulls the stomach down and to the left, while the surgeon's fourth arm pulls everything to the right.

The surgeon dissects the space between the gastroesophageal junction and the left crus of the diaphragm, maneuvering the stomach towards the patient's left with assistance. The fourth arm is positioned under the esophagus at the gastroesophageal junction, preparing to place a Penrose drain around the esophagus.

The surgeon places a Penrose drain around the gastroesophageal junction, using it as a gentle retractor to gain length on the esophagus. A small slit is cut into the drain, allowing the assistant to hold it up, facilitating enhanced visualization and access into the posterior mediastinum.

The surgeon punctures into the right pleural space, which is visibly entered. The vessel sealer extend is used to divide the connective tissue attachments between the esophagus and the mediastinal structures.

The surgeon uses the vessel sealer extend to dissect connective tissue around the esophagus, aiming to achieve three to four centimeters of esophageal length below the diaphragm without tension. The posterior vagus nerve is visible, incorporated in the Penrose drain. The fourth arm holds the Penrose drain while the assistant pulls down on the gastric fat pad, allowing the surgeon to encircle and free the esophagus from its attachments.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm and assistance to expose the surgical field with a thirty-degree down camera view. Connective tissue attachments are being divided near the esophagus, with careful attention to avoid entering the left pleural space, which could complicate visualization due to the diaphragm's movement.

The surgeon divides omental fat adhered to the underside of the diaphragm near the left crus, exposing the confluence of the crura posteriorly. The area is cleaned to prepare for seating bioabsorbable mesh flush against the diaphragm and placing stitches.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer extend to dissect the plane between the gastric fat pad and the stomach, excising the fat pad to improve visualization of the anatomy, particularly the angle of His. The esophagus is shown with good length below the diaphragm, without tension.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the stomach wall towards the angle of His, being cautious not to compromise the anterior vagus nerve, which is located on the left side of the esophagus and angles down anteriorly. The gastric fat pad has been removed, providing a clear view of the angle of His, which is positioned below the diaphragm without tension.

The surgeon uses the robotic system to mobilize the esophagus, gaining an additional centimeter of length while maintaining a safe margin to protect the vagus nerve. The descending aorta is visible, and the surgeon uses the fourth arm of the robot to expose the area, ensuring the vagus nerve remains lateral and protected.

The surgeon manipulates the esophagus to gain an additional one to two centimeters of length, examining it from a 360-degree perspective. The descending aorta is visible below, with some omental fat obstructing the view. The surgeon inspects the gastroesophageal (GE) junction and identifies the angle of His, noting connective tissue causing slight puckering. The vagus nerve is visible curling to the left, and the surgeon carefully avoids it while extending the esophagus on the left lateral side.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the Penrose drain to retract tissue while exposing the left crus of the diaphragm. The omentum is held away by an assistant. Simple stitches using 0 Ethibond are placed around the crus, preserving the peritoneal cell layer to prevent tearing. The Vessel Sealer Extend is used as a needle driver and cutter, facilitating suture placement and cutting. The table-side assistant uses a TK knot pusher to secure the knots without tension.

The surgeon reinforces the hernia repair with bioabsorbable mesh, preserving the peritoneal layer over the crura. The Penrose drain is held up with the fourth arm while the surgeon feeds the stitch to the table-side assistant. The second stitch is placed, and a snug closure around the esophagus is created without using a bougie, avoiding potential injury. A third posterior stitch is applied.

The surgeon reinforces the posterior stitches with mesh to prevent hernia recurrence, using the Vessel Sealer Extend to hold the omental fat away. An anterior stitch is placed to achieve a snug closure around the esophagus, ensuring three to four centimeters of length without tension.

The surgeon removes fluid to expose the diaphragm, preparing to place bioabsorbable Acell mesh made from pig bladder. The mesh is laid in a U-shaped fashion around the posterior crura and tacked to the underside of the diaphragm using two-o Ethibond stitches. The table-side assistant uses a TK knot pusher to secure the knots.

The surgeon ties down the posterior anchoring stitch on the diaphragm's crus, securing the mesh in place. The mesh is visibly annealed to the diaphragm's underside, appearing well-positioned. The angle of His and the gastroesophageal junction are clearly visible, with three to four centimeters of length available, indicating readiness to begin the fundoplication procedure.

The surgeon pulls the tissue to ensure sufficient mobility and prepares to place the first stitch at the angle of His, between the stomach and esophagus at the gastroesophageal junction. The stitch incorporates a portion of the mesh and diaphragm, as part of a partial anterior fundoplication and gastropexy. The procedure uses two O-ethabond sutures.

The surgeon performs an anterior fundoplication and gastropexy, anchoring the stomach below the diaphragm to prevent reflux and recurrence. The diaphragm is grasped as part of the procedure, with stitches placed to secure the wrap without taking down the short gastric vessels, avoiding potential ischemia. The wrap is completed with four or five stitches, ensuring it lays flat.

The surgeon completes a partial anterior fundoplication, retrieving omental fat and hernia sac contents through a small bag. An esophagus scope is inserted to inspect the repair internally, ensuring the gastroesophageal junction is positioned below the diaphragm. The stomach is suctioned, concluding the procedure.
